coredata.dc:title: Lanthanide complexes functionalized carbon dot nanocomposites as lubricant additives for improving tribological performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xinwei
Wang, Qinhuan
Kong, Haoran
Li, Yuting
Xin, Xiulan
Wang, Chunfeng
Liang, Qingyan
Wang, Yu
coredata.dc:description: A novel nanocomposite, CDs-La-EDTA, was synthesized by bonding carbon dots to the functional sites of rare earth complexes, and its tribological characteristics were evaluated as lubricant additives. The results showed that the mean friction coefficient and the wear scar diameter of lubricating oil containing 0.15 wt% CDs-La-EDTA were reduced by 14% and 22%, respectively, compared with those of the base oil. Furthermore, the dual lubrication mechanism of the nanocomposite was proposed. The rolling or sliding effect of rare earth nanofibrous rods and the mending effect of carbon dot particles contributed to excellent lubrication performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposites
Carbon materials
Rare earth complexes
Wear and tribology
Coordination chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of pure CDs, inset: the size distribution; (b) SEM image of La-EDTA; (c) TEM image and (d) EDS elemental mapping images of the CDs-La-EDTA composites.
(a) FT-IR results of CDs-La-EDTA, CDs and La-EDTA; XPS spectra of CDs-La-EDTA nanocomposites C 1 s (b), O 1 s (c) and La 3d (d).

(a) Curves of the friction coefficient; (b) Mean friction coefficient and wear scar diameter of lubricating oil containing various concentrations of CDs-La-EDTA; (c) Histograms of the mean friction coefficient and WSD lubricated by the base oil, 0.15 wt% CDs modified oil, and 0.15 wt% CDs-La-EDTA modified oil; (d), (e) Optical images of the wear scar on the steel ball and (f), (g) The 3D topographies of wear surfaces on steel discs(f), (g) Lubricated by the base oil and 0.15 wt% CDs-La-EDTA modified oil.

head.ce:title: Lanthanide complexes functionalized carbon dot nanocomposites as lubricant additives for improving tribological performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Multiphase Complex Systems, Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Multiphase Complex Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Yuchai Machinery Co., Ltd, Yulin 537005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nanocomposite, CDs-La-EDTA, was synthesized by bonding carbon dots to the functional sites of rare earth complexes, and its tribological characteristics were evaluated as lubricant additives. The results showed that the mean friction coefficient and the wear scar diameter of lubricating oil containing 0.15 wt% CDs-La-EDTA were reduced by 14% and 22%, respectively, compared with those of the base oil. Furthermore, the dual lubrication mechanism of the nanocomposite was proposed. The rolling or sliding effect of rare earth nanofibrous rods and the mending effect of carbon dot particles contributed to excellent lubrication performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials such as graphene, fullerenes, and carbon nanotubes have attracted great attention in the lubricant industry [1–2]. As a family of zero-dimensional nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) present upper advantages as lubricant additives due to their environmental friendliness, designability, and low-cost precursors [3–4]. However, the inferior dispersion of nanoparticles in oil solutions leads to a decline in the stability of the whole system, which has been a barrier in the field of tribology. In addition, the tribological properties of rare earth complexes as lubricating oil additives are also quite considerable. Based on the theory of coordination chemistry, the central atoms of the rare earth complexes are separated to achieve atomic dispersion in the oil solvent, which greatly improves the stability and lubricity of the lubricating oil [5]. For instance, Li et al. used reduced graphene oxide as a carrier of Eu(Phen)(OA)3 particles and synthesized new lubricant RGECs with decent dispersibility and wear resistance by hydrothermal reactions [6]. The synergistic effect of CDs and rare earth complexes is able to provide a key strategy for the development of novel nanocomposites, which could further optimize the lubricating properties of carbon-based nano-additives.

In this work, {[La(EDTA)(H2O)]2}n (La-EDTA) was prepared by a hydrothermal method, in which each La was nine-coordinated by two nitrogen and six oxygen atoms from three EDTA molecules, as well as one water molecule [7]. Then it was compounded with CDs to successfully develop a highly efficient lubricant additive, named CDs-La-EDTA.

The CDs-La-EDTA nanocomposites were synthesized by the following procedures. The La-EDTA solution was added to the CD dispersion described in supporting materials at a volume ratio of 2:1, followed by the microwave-assisted hydrothermal reaction at 100 ℃ and 700 W power for 1 h. Finally, the obtained solution was spray-dried to obtain CDs-La-EDTA powder, and the mass fraction of pure CDs was 65.07 wt% in the composites.

The prepared CDs-La-EDTA nanocomposites were dispersed in the base oil at different concentrations for at least 80 cycles under a pressure of 1300 bar using a homogenizer. The tribological properties of CDs-La-EDTA modified lubricating oil were determined on a ball-on-disc tribometer (SRV, Bruker) in reciprocating sliding mode. The test balls and discs were made of 100Cr6 steel with a hardness of 66 HRC. Tests were carried out at a load of 200 N, a frequency of 50 Hz, and a sliding stroke of 1 mm at 50 °C. The rheological behavior of the base oil and CDs-La-EDTA suspensions was evaluated in a rotational rheometer at 25 ℃.

Fig. 1 a showed that all particles of CDs possessed quasi-spherical shapes and uniform size distributions by TEM. The average particle size of CDs was approximately 5.1 nm. Fig. 1b showed the SEM morphology of the La-EDTA composites after freeze-drying. La-EDTA complexes exhibited a fibrous structure, indicating the synthesis of one-dimensional coordination polymers [7]. The TEM morphology of the CDs-La-EDTA composites as shown in Fig. 1c, also exhibited a fibrous structure corresponding to the La-EDTA structure (Fig. 1b). EDS elemental mapping revealed the homogenous distribution of C, O, and La over the entire region (Fig. 1d).

The XRD pattern of CDs-La-EDTA showed a wide diffraction peak at 20.5°, which was consistent with the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. S3) [8]. The characteristic peaks of CDs and La-EDTA nanomaterials were perfectly reflected in the pattern of CDs-La-EDTA, implying that synthesized CDs-La-EDTA composites contained both crystalline carbon nuclei and polymeric fiber.

In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a), CDs-La-EDTA had the same functional groups as CDs, which indicated that the physicochemical activity of this compound was similar to that of CDs. The peaks at 1597 nm and 1415 nm were assigned to symmetrical and asymmetrical vibrations of ligand COO¯ groups [9]. Comparing the FT-IR spectra of CDs-La-EDTA and La-EDTA, the vibration of COO¯ groups in La-EDTA at 1597 nm and 1415 nm were stronger than that of CDs-La-EDTA, which indicated that CDs interacted with La-EDTA and weakened the COO¯ vibration.

The chemical composition of the CDs-La-EDTA organic composites was characterized by XPS (Fig. 2b). The three characteristic peaks of 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 287.6 eV in the C1s spectrum were related to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO, respectively. The O1s spectrum revealed three fitted peaks (533.0 eV, 532.3 eV, and 531.7 eV), which were assigned to CO, CO, and La-O, respectively (Fig. 2c) [10]. The XPS spectrum of La3d showed the ionic characteristic peak of La in the + 3 valence state (Fig. 2d). The results above indicated that the CDs-La-EDTA were synthesized successfully.

Fig. 3 a showed the friction curves of the base oil and CDs-La-EDTA nanofluids at 0.05 ∼ 0.30 wt% concentrations during a 15-minute friction test. The friction of coefficient (COF) curves reached a relatively stable state without a long interfacial running-in period, implying that the lubricant had a better film-forming property. The mean friction coefficient and wear scar diameter (WSD) of lubricating oil were provided in Fig. 3b. When the concentration of CDs-La-EDTA was 0.15 wt%, the mean COF of the modified lubricating oil reached the minimum value of 0.126, which corresponded to a decrease of 14 % in comparison with that of the base oil. Meanwhile, the WSD of lubricants containing CDs-La-EDTA nano-additives at 0.15 wt% concentration also reached the minimum (0.37 mm) and was decreased by 22 % compared with that of the base oil. These results indicated that the CDs-La-EDTA exhibited outstanding friction-reducing and anti-wear performances. To further explore the lubricating role of La-EDTA on CDs-La-EDTA, the tribological properties of the oil with 0.15 wt% CDs were also displayed in Fig. 3c. The results showed that the modification of CDs by La-EDTA can effectively improve the friction-reducing and anti-wear properties of composites. In addition, the optical images of the wear scar on the ball and the 3D topographies of the wear track on the disc lubricated by CDs-La-EDTA were smaller and shallower than those of the base oil, which more intuitively reflected the excellent tribological behaviors of CDs-La-EDTA nanocomposites.

The rheology of the fluid is an important factor affecting the lubrication performance of lubricating oil [11]. Fig. S5 showed the rheological behavior of the base oil and CDs-La-EDTA suspensions at 0.15 wt% and 0.30 wt% concentrations, respectively. The viscosity of the fluids remained a low constant with the change in shear rate (10 ∼ 100 S-1), which indicated that the behavior of the base oil and the suspensions was Newtonian. Thefilm-formingproperty wasbetterduetothe higherviscosityoftheCDs-La-EDTAsuspensions. The nanocomposites introduced a large number of disordered fibrous structures to the oil solution. The N and O coordination atoms provided by the organic component EDTA of the nanofiber were able to form new coordination covalent bonds with the Fe atoms on the disc, resulting in the nanofibrous rods adhering to the friction surface. The nanofibrous rods gradually had a consistent rolling or sliding direction under the action of shear force, ensuring the stability of the oil film and making the friction coefficient fluctuate slightly. Due to the action of mechanical extrusion and heat caused by friction, CDs on the nanofibrous rods constantly fell off, and then the falling CDs behaved a mending effect by repairing the cracks, scratches, and pocks on the wear scars during the friction process. In Fig. S6, the roughness of the disc surface is approximately 0.05 μm (Sa = 0.055 μm), and the size of the nanoparticles is as small as 5 nm. There was no condition of free rolling or sliding on the wear interface for CDs. Thus, the CDs were not able to play the role of ball-bearing and polishing on the rubbing surface, only to mend the grooves of wear scars, owing to their relatively small particle sizes.

In summary, a new composite CDs-La-EDTA has been proposed through a facile and scalable method, successfully bonding CDs to the surface functional sites of La-EDTA polymers based on coordination chemistry theory. When the CDs-La-EDTA nanocomposites were used as lubricating oil additives, the mean friction coefficient and wear scar diameter were decreased by 14 % and 22 %, respectively. Under the synergistic effect of the CDs and the La-EDTA complexes, the friction and wear were significantly reduced due to the rolling or sliding effect of rare earth nanofibrous rods and the mending effect of CDs. The CDs-La-EDTA composite has been demonstrated as an excellent candidate additive for lubricating oil, which shows that the future of CDs-based lubricant additives remains promising.

Xinwei Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Qinhuan Wang: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Haoran Kong: Software, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Yuting Li: Writing – review & editing. Xiulan Xin: Resources. Chunfeng Wang: Resources. Qingyan Liang: Resources. Yu Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Yu WANG reports financial support was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China. Yu WANG reports financial support was provided by the Fund of the Key Research and Development Program of Guangxi. Yu WANG has patent pending to 2022105886047.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875256), and the Fund of the Key Research and Development Program of Guangxi (No. Guike AB20297032).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.133697.
